summary:
The fall of Nanjing in July 1864 ended the [[event:2543|Taiping Rebellion]] and the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom that had controlled the city as its capital for eleven years. The final campaign was prosecuted primarily by Zeng Guofan's Xiang (Hunan) Army, which methodically reduced the Taiping-held territories after years of grueling warfare.

By 1863 the Taiping kingdom had been progressively squeezed by the regional armies of Zeng Guofan and his protégé Li Hongzhang, who commanded the Huai Army. The Ever Victorious Army of Western-officered Chinese troops, commanded after the death of Frederick Townsend Ward by the British officer Charles Gordon, had also been successful in suppressing Taiping power around Shanghai. The Taiping leader Hong Xiuquan became increasingly erratic in his final years, issuing religious proclamations and refusing to negotiate when military options remained open.

Nanjing was surrounded and the final assault came in July 1864. Hong Xiuquan had died in late June — probably from poisoning, possibly suicide — before the city fell. When the Xiang Army broke through the walls, the remaining Taiping forces and much of the civilian population were killed in an orgy of violence. Contemporary accounts described the streets running with blood. The young Taiping heir was captured and executed.

The victory established the power and prestige of Zeng Guofan and the regional military commanders who had saved the dynasty, though it left the Qing government heavily dependent on these same commanders and their regional armies — a structural shift that would have profound consequences for the fragmentation of central authority in the late Qing and early Republican period.
